perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees to keep confidential and refrain from disclosing any an d all of the INFORMATION, and to take all necessary and reasonable steps to prevent unauthorized disclosure or use of any and all of the INFORMATION.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT will endeavor to keep the INFORMATION confidential and will take steps it considers appropriate to prevent unauthorized disclosure or use of the INFORMATION.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "agrees to keep confidential" with "will endeavor to keep the INFORMATION confidential" weakens the obligation. "Endeavor" implies an attempt, not a guarantee of confidentiality. Similarly, "all necessary and reasonable steps" is replaced by "steps it considers appropriate", making the preventative measures subjective and discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The modified text replaces this with "steps it considers appropriate," which may not meet the standard of 'reasonable under the circumstances' as required by California law, potentially leaving the trade secret unprotected.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original contract language creates a strong obligation to maintain confidentiality and prevent unauthorized disclosure, using the phrase "agrees to keep confidential" and requiring "all necessary and reasonable steps." The changed text weakens this obligation significantly by using "will endeavor to keep the INFORMATION confidential" and taking "steps it considers appropriate." According to the Law Explanation, CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The scraped law snippet 1, California Civil Code 3426.1, defines "improper means" to include breach of a duty to maintain secrecy. The altered language potentially fails to meet the standard of reasonable effort, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT shall not be liable for disclosure or use of INFORMATION only if, and only to the extent that, said INFORMATION was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed by OWNER, or was known to and recorded in writing by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNE R, or is received from a third party or passes into the public domain without breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT may not be liable for disclosure or use of INFORMATION if said INFORMATION was seemingly in the public domain, or was possibly known to RECIPIENT prior to disclosure, or is allegedly received from a third party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall not be liable" to "may not be liable" introduces uncertainty. Replacing "was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed" with "was seemingly in the public domain" weakens the requirement to prove public domain status. Altering "known to and recorded in writing" to "possibly known to RECIPIENT" removes the written record requirement and adds ambiguity. Lastly, changing "is received from a third party" to "is allegedly received from a third party" adds doubt to the source of information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, misappropriation can be based on use of improperly acquired information. The original clause had clear exceptions to liability if the information was already publicly available, previously known and documented, or received from a third party. The modified text weakens the requirements for these exceptions with ambiguous terms like "seemingly," "possibly," and "allegedly," making it difficult to determine when the RECIPIENT'S actions are truly exempt from liability under CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modification creates a significant ambiguity regarding the RECIPIENT's liability for disclosure or use of information, potentially conflicting with CUTSA. The original text provided clear exceptions to liability, whereas the changed text uses vague terms like 'seemingly,' 'possibly,' and 'allegedly,' weakening the requirements to meet those exceptions. This ambiguity could make it difficult to determine if the RECIPIENT's actions are truly exempt from liability. Snippet 1, referencing CIV 3426.1, defines 'Misappropriation' as the disclosure or use of a trade secret 'without express or implied consent'. By introducing ambiguity into the exceptions, the changed text makes it harder to prove whether there was consent or if the information was indeed a trade secret, potentially creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees to execute all papers and to perform such other proper acts as OWNER may deem necessary to secure for OWNER or its designee the rights herein assigned.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT agrees to consider executing papers and performing acts OWNER feels are helpful to secure the rights herein assigned.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "agrees to execute all papers and to perform such other proper acts as OWNER may deem necessary" to "agrees to consider executing papers and performing acts OWNER feels are helpful" introduces discretion. The word "consider" softens the obligation to execute documents, and the term "helpful" is much weaker than "necessary." The recipient has no firm obligation to comply.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California contract law regarding good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By changing the language to a mere agreement to "consider" something "helpful," RECIPIENT is given wide latitude and discretion, which could be used to frustrate the purpose of the original assignment. This goes against the spirit of California law that mandates that both parties must act in good faith to carry out the intent of the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1655. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1655. Stipulations which are necessary to make a contract reasonable, or conformable to usage, are implied, in respect to matters concerning which the contract manifests no contrary intention. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the recipient's obligation to secure the owner's rights. The original text states the recipient "agrees to execute all papers and to perform such other proper acts as OWNER may deem necessary." The modified text softens this obligation to "agrees to consider executing papers and performing acts OWNER feels are helpful." This change introduces discretion and potentially allows the recipient to not fully comply with the original intent. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Code, CIV 1655, states that stipulations necessary to make a contract reasonable are implied, unless the contract manifests a contrary intention. By changing the language to a mere agreement to "consider" something "helpful," the recipient is given wide latitude that undermines the good faith expected in a contract and may frustrate the purpose of the assignment.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT hereby acknowledges that OWNER shall retain all right, title, and interest in all trademarks, trade dress, and good will that results from the INFORMATION or any use or offer to sell thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT acknowledges that OWNER intends to retain rights, title, and interest in trademarks, trade dress and good will that arguably results from the INFORMATION.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that OWNER retains all rights. The changed text weakens this by stating OWNER "intends to retain rights," making it an intention rather than a guaranteed right. Using the word "arguably" also introduces doubt about whether the trademarks, trade dress, and goodwill are actually a result of the INFORMATION.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act protects trademark rights, which stem from actual use and ownership. The modified text creates ambiguity around ownership. By using 'intends to retain' and 'arguably results,' the contract fails to clearly assign trademark rights derived from the confidential information, undermining OWNER’s ability to enforce those rights under the Lanham Act. If the rights are not clearly defined through contractual language, it becomes more difficult to prove ownership and enforce trademark protection against infringement, thereby hindering OWNER's potential legal claims.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. The United States, all agencies and instrume
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly assigns ownership rights to the OWNER. The changed text uses phrases like "intends to retain" and "arguably results," which introduce ambiguity about the OWNER's actual ownership of trademarks, trade dress, and goodwill. The Lanham Act, as suggested by the Law Explanation and supported by the scraped snippets (15 USC 1114), aims to protect trademark rights stemming from actual use and ownership. By creating ambiguity around the ownership and the source of these rights, the revised contract weakens the OWNER's ability to enforce these rights under the Lanham Act. The original text clearly assigned ownership and the change compromises this assignment, creating a legal contradiction under the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.
